Well, let's get started actually creating a sales letter using raised pastor framework. And I'm going to show you how I do this. When I go out to the writing shed, I sit down, I've got to write a sales letter today. I actually just take a document, take an empty Word document or pages document, and I type out the pastor framework so that I can fill in the blanks. So the first thing I do is I just type out problem, and I make it really bold so that I know, okay, I'm going to write the problem in that little section. And then I'm going to amplify the problem. And then I'm going to put either a story or some sort of solution. And transformation and testimony. Then the offer, then the response. And that's really it. Now I have all these sections, kind of even them out here, that I know I need to fill with text. So let's say my client is the pet store down the street. So we'll just say this client is Belmont neighborhood pet shop. And we need to understand, of course, this is going to come from really listening to your client and understanding what they're trying to sell and what they're trying to move. You want to sit down with the client and say, you know, what is it that you're trying to sell? What product are you trying to move? Who are you talking to? What is the target market? I'm assuming you already understand all that so that we can fill in the pastor framework sales letter with the information that you already have. So let's say I went down to Belmont neighborhood pet shop, and I talked to them, and they're wanting to sell more of this particular dog food that is for adult dogs or senior dogs, right? So I'm going to find out all sorts of information, and then I'm going to choose a problem to highlight. So here this problem is, you know, dogs as they get older, they tend to get arthritis because they're eating too much grain. So I'm just going to, this is kind of like one of those cooking shows where we've done the work before and for the sake of time where we're going to plug it in. But I'm going to put in this text, as our dogs get older, they need less and less grain in their diets. Grain can lead to arthritis, bone spurs, and other painful conditions dogs suffer from in their later years. So right off the bat, I've started with a problem. And if anybody receives this letter or this email, and they read that, and they do have a dog that's limping, they're going to go, wow, this is for me, and they're going to keep reading. So you really want to just start with the problem. It's the Stamen story. Story starts with a character who gets into trouble. And so as soon as you open with that problem, you've hooked the reader. Now the great thing about Ray's framework is he says, present a problem and then amplify the problem, which is exactly what happens in movies. They present a character that has a problem, and then they make the problem a lot worse. It's kind of like when you're fishing and you throw out a hook and you get a little nibble. You sense that nibble on your line and you jerk the rod a little bit in order to set the hook in the fish. It's really similar with writing a good sales letter. Present a problem, then make the problem worse, amplify the problem, and really set the hook. So I've created this problem, or I've talked about a problem that the customer has. Their dog is limping, it's suffering from arthritis. Now I want to amplify the problem. So let's see how I did that. Basically, I just took the problem and I described how bad it can really be. Not only this, but a high-grain diet can take a year or more off a dog's life. And even their final years can lack the enjoyment healthy dogs often experience. Well, we just took a problem and we amplify the problem. And now we want to move to the second part where we start relieving the tension in the sales letter. You know, sales letters, movies, stories, even music, a lot of times it's about creating tension and relieving the tension. Creating tension and relieving the tension. And the sales letter works the same way. We create a problem, we make it worse, and we begin to relieve the problem. So under story and solution, I chose a little bit more of a solution way of going about this. But it's fine that when we put dogs on a diet consisting of Royal Canons adult formula, they thrive. So we've relieved some of that tension. And then we continue relieving the tension. Royal Canons adult formula mixed with a fish oil supplement and Dasequin, both proven to slow the advancement of arthritis in dogs, contains no grain and no inflammatory is allowing dogs to look and feel their best even in the later years. So there we've done it. We've had problem, amplified problem, relief of problem. And now Ray wants us to put in a transformation or testimony. So this is where we get really personal about one of our clients and we show them exactly how this can work. So I just tell a story of a client. One of our frequent customers, a chocolate lab named Dexter, started walking better in only three weeks on this simple protocol. His owner had stopped taking him swimming because his limp had gotten so bad. But we just heard yesterday Dexter is back in the water and enjoying this beautiful summer. There we go. Now this would be kind of a climactic scene if we were riding a screenplay, a scene that shows that all the tension has been resolved. And then of course we want to create an offer for the customer. Now they know, we've set a path for them that they can experience this great thing. And now we're going to tell them how, if they respond to our offer. So we are now combining Royal Canons Adult Formula with a five pound bag of Dasequin. In parentheses, dogs love the flavor and they think of it as a treat. With a fish oil supplement, all for a $20 total savings. A 60 pound dog will take about a month to eat this much food. So this amounts to a $240 savings this year alone. There's my offer. I offer to relieve the tension that you're no doubt experiencing. And now we want to ask them to respond. We want to call them to action in our final paragraph. So we're going to say, we want you to come on down, right? Come down to Belmont Neighborhood Pet Shop today and pick up your first month's supply. Your older dog is going to love the taste of Royal Canons and they're going to love you even more for helping them feel better and live longer. When you come, mention this letter and we will make sure you get your full discount. Don't delay any longer. Come on in today. And then of course, best or sincerely, whatever, and the owner's signature or whoever it is that you're riding on behalf of. That's a really good sales letter. When we remove, of course, these headings, you see that it just flows really, really well. And then we'll go to section after section and it creates a great narrative sales letter that will get a response. Now what I would do if I were in your shoes, I would take this letter. I'd probably let it sit for about 24 hours and then I'd come back in and tighten the letter up. I'd get rid of as many words as possible, really make it efficient. I also might bold some of the texts that I want them to really understand or emotionally highlight. You want to bold a little bit of negative, a little bit of positive, and of course you want to bold the offer at the end. And then you're going to have a really great sales letter that works. And the great thing is you can take the framework that I just used, use it for a thousand different clients, use it to sell a thousand different products, and nobody actually realizes that you're using the framework because their brain is just designed to walk this narrative path that you were creating for them. So take the pastor framework that Ray taught you. Look at my example, study those, and you'll have the formula for writing great sales letter, letter after letter, client after client, product after product. And the more you do it, the better you're going to get and the better the response is going to be to the sales letters that you create.
